source_rationale: The matrix category was further divided into five populations based on their transcriptional profiles: i) cuticle (KRT32, KRT35), ii) cortex (KRT31), iii) medulla (BAMBI), iv) inner root sheath (IRS) (KRT71, KRT28) and v) proliferative cells, expressing high levels of TOP2A and MKI67 genes (Figure 1E).

The matrix category was further divided into five populations based on their transcriptional profiles: i) cuticle (KRT32, KRT35), ii) cortex (KRT31), iii) medulla (BAMBI), iv) inner root sheath (IRS) (KRT71, KRT28) and v) proliferative cells, expressing high levels of TOP2A and MKI67 genes (Figure 1E).

The matrix category was further divided into five populations based on their transcriptional profiles: i) cuticle (KRT32, KRT35), ii) cortex (KRT31), iii) medulla (BAMBI), iv) inner root sheath (IRS) (KRT71, KRT28) and v) proliferative cells, expressing high levels of TOP2A and MKI67 genes (Figure 1E).

The non-matrix category also regroups five populations: i) hair follicle stem cells (HF-SCs) expressing high levels of KRT15, COL17A1, DIO2 and TCEAL2, ii)basal interfollicular epidermis (IFE) expressing KRT15 and COL17A1 but lacking DIO2 and TCEAL2 expression iii) granular and spinous IFE expressing SPINK5 and LY6D, iv) outer root sheath cells (ORS) expressing cells expressing KRT16, KRT6B and KRT6C, and (v) sebocytes expressing CLMP and PPARG (Figure 1F).

HF-SCs expressed a core set of stemness-associated genes, including key transcription factors such as SOX9, LHX2, and RUNX1, which help maintain stem cell quiescence and an undifferentiated state.

Interestingly, their differentiated progeny retained subsets of these markers: IFE basal cells maintained SOX9, RUNX1, and COL17A1, while ORS cells retained SOX9, LHX2, and FGF18 (Figure 2D).

In HS patients, HF-SCs displayed an inflammatory phenotype, as evidenced by an enrichment in HLA class I molecule, MIF and IFITM3 (Figure 3A).
In HS patients, HF-SCs displayed an inflammatory phenotype, as evidenced by an enrichment in HLA class I molecule, MIF and IFITM3 (Figure 3A).
In contrast, HF-SCs from healthy donors showed higher expression of genes involved in DNA repair, such as DDIT4, and apoptosis pathway suggesting a more quiescent, homeostatic state (Figure 3A).

A similar shift towards inflammation was observed in IFE basal cells from HS patients, which exhibited increased expression of inflammatory mediators such as CCL2 and S100A9, along with a type I interferon signature (IFI27, IFITM3) (Figure 3B).

A similar shift towards inflammation was observed in IFE basal cells from HS patients, which exhibited increased expression of inflammatory mediators such as CCL2 and S100A9, along with a type I interferon signature (IFI27, IFITM3) (Figure 3B).

Conversely, genes involved in cell cycle regulation (DUSP1, KLF6) and epithelial barrier integrity (CTGF, CLDN1) were downregulated in HS patients, potentially contributing to impaired epithelial homeostasis (Figure 3B).

ORS cells overexpressed MIF and S100A9, consistent with immune activation (Figure 3C).
ORS cells overexpressed MIF and S100A9, consistent with immune activation (Figure 3C).

Notably, we observed upregulation of LGALS7 (galectin-7), a lectin predominantly expressed in stratified epithelia (10), and ARF5, a small GTPase involved in splicing, ribosome synthesis, and intracellular trafficking (11) (Figure 3C).

Notably, we observed upregulation of LGALS7 (galectin-7), a lectin predominantly expressed in stratified epithelia (10), and ARF5, a small GTPase involved in splicing, ribosome synthesis, and intracellular trafficking (11) (Figure 3C).
By contrast, ORS cells from healthy donors expressed higher levels of DUSP1 and DDIT4, genes crucial for cell cycle control and cellular stress response.
By contrast, ORS cells from healthy donors expressed higher levels of DUSP1 and DDIT4, genes crucial for cell cycle control and cellular stress response.
Notably, expression of IL1B and TNF levels was significantly elevated in HS macrophages (p = 0.058; p < 0.001, respectively) (Figure 4D), while IL6 expression was undetectable (Figure S3A).
Notably, expression of IL1B and TNF levels was significantly elevated in HS macrophages (p = 0.058; p < 0.001, respectively) (Figure 4D), while IL6 expression was undetectable (Figure S3A).
CD4+ T cells exhibited a cytotoxic phenotype, characterized by elevated expression of GZMA and KLRB1, which was confirmed by immunofluorescence (Figure 4E-G).
CD4+ T cells exhibited a cytotoxic phenotype, characterized by elevated expression of GZMA and KLRB1, which was confirmed by immunofluorescence (Figure 4E-G).
Furthermore, CD8+ T cells in HS patients exhibited significantly higher expression of IFNG, GZMB, and PRF1 compared to healthy donors, consistent with cytotoxic activity (Figure S3B-D).
Furthermore, CD8+ T cells in HS patients exhibited significantly higher expression of IFNG, GZMB, and PRF1 compared to healthy donors, consistent with cytotoxic activity (Figure S3B-D).
